message.content: Review the v1 milestone. is anything remaining to be done? If not, mark it closed.

input.command: gh api repos/:owner/:repo/milestones --jq '.[] | select(.title | test("v1"; "i")) | {number, title, state, open_issues, closed_issues, description}'

content.content: {"closed_issues":6,"description":"Core companion app — setup flow, Today page, queue engine, Hevy sync, and progress tracking. Ships a usable single-program experience.","number":1,"open_issues":0,"state":"open","title":"v1: Core App"}

toolUseResult.stdout: {"closed_issues":6,"description":"Core companion app — setup flow, Today page, queue engine, Hevy sync, and progress tracking. Ships a usable single-program experience.","number":1,"open_issues":0,"state":"open","title":"v1: Core App"}

content.text: No open issues remaining — 6/6 closed. Closing it now.

input.command: gh api repos/:owner/:repo/milestones/1 -X PATCH -f state=closed --jq '{title, state, open_issues, closed_issues}'

content.text: **v1: Core App** milestone is now closed. All 6 issues were already completed — setup flow, Today page, queue engine, Hevy sync, and progress tracking all shipped.

message.content: What's the next milestone, and what's the gh commands to manage milestones?

content.content:
{"closed_issues":0,"number":2,"open_issues":5,"state":"open","title":"v2: Multi-Program \u0026 Sync"}
{"closed_issues":0,"number":3,"open_issues":4,"state":"open","title":"v3: Intelligence \u0026 Personalization"}

toolUseResult.stdout:
{"closed_issues":0,"number":2,"open_issues":5,"state":"open","title":"v2: Multi-Program \u0026 Sync"}

content.text:
**Next up: v2: Multi-Program & Sync** — 5 open issues, 0 closed.

---

**Useful `gh` milestone commands:**

```bash
# List milestones
gh api repos/:owner/:repo/milestones

# Create milestone
gh api repos/:owner/:repo/milestones -X POST \
  -f title="v4: Whatever" -f description="Details"

# Update milestone (by number)
gh api repos/:owner/:repo/milestones/2 -X PATCH \
  -f title="New Title" -f description="Updated"

# Close milestone
gh api repos/:owner/:repo/milestones/2 -X PATCH -f state=closed

# Reopen milestone
gh api repos/:owner/:repo/milestones/2 -X PATCH -f state=open

# Delete milestone
gh api repos/:owner/:repo/milestones/2 -X DELETE

# List issues in a milestone
gh issue list --milestone "v2: Multi-Program & Sync"

# Set due date
gh api repos/:owner/:repo/milestones/2 -X PATCH \
  -f due_on="2026-04-15T00:00:00Z"
```

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /Users/ryanr/.claude/skills/boris

# Boris Cherny's Claude Code Workflow Tips

> **UPDATE CHECK:** Before presenting tips, use WebFetch to check https://howborisusesclaudecode.com/api/version for the latest version. Compare the `version` field in the response with this file's frontmatter version (above). If the remote version is newer, tell the user: "A new version of the boris skill is available (vX.X.X). Update with: `mkdir -p ~/.claude/skills/boris && curl -L -o ~/.claude/skills/boris/SKILL.md https://howborisusesclaudecode.com/api/install`" — then continue normally. If versions match or the check fails, skip silently.

**53 tips** across 41 topics, sourced from Boris Cherny (creator of Claude Code) and the Claude Code team at Anthropic. All tips are contained in this file — do not fetch from the website. Remember: everyone's setup is different. Experiment to see what works for you!

**Parts:** The tips were shared across 7 threads:
- **Part 1** (Jan 2, 2026, 13 tips): Sections 1–14 — parallel execution, web/mobile, Opus, CLAUDE.md, @.claude, plan mode, slash commands, subagents, hooks, permissions, MCP, long-running tasks, verification
- **Part 2** (Jan 31, 2026, 10 tips): Sections 1, 3, 4, 5, 12, 10, 11, 6, 9, 15 — deeper dives on parallel work, plan mode, CLAUDE.md, skills, bug fixing, prompting, terminal setup, subagents, data/analytics, learning
- **Part 3** (Feb 11, 2026, 12 tips): Sections 16–27 — terminal config, effort level, plugins, custom agents, permissions management, sandboxing, status line, keybindings, hooks (advanced), spinner verbs, output styles, customize everything
- **Part 4** (Feb 20, 2026, 5 tips): Section 28 — built-in worktree support (CLI, Desktop, subagents, custom agents, non-git VCS)
- **Part 5** (Feb 27, 2026, 2 tips): Sections 29–30 — /simplify and /batch
- **Part 6** (Mar 7–10, 2026, 3 tips): Sections 31–33 — /loop for scheduled recurring tasks, code review agents, /btw for mid-task questions
- **Part 7** (Mar 13, 2026, 8 tips): Sections 34–41 — /effort max, remote control sessions, voice mode, setup scripts, session naming, /color, PostCompact hook

---

## 1. Parallel Execution

### Run Multiple Claude Sessions in Parallel
The single biggest productivity unlock. Spin up 3-5 git worktrees at once, each running its own Claude session.

```bash
# Create a worktree
git worktree add .claude/worktrees/my-worktree origin/main

# Start Claude in it
cd .claude/worktrees/my-worktree && claude
```

**Why worktrees over checkouts:** The Claude Code team prefers worktrees - it's why native support was built into the Claude Desktop app.

**Pro tips:**
- Name your worktrees and set up shell aliases (za, zb, zc) to hop between them in one keystroke
- Have a dedicated "analysis" worktree just for reading logs and running BigQuery
- Use iTerm2/terminal notifications to know when any Claude needs attention
- Color-code and name your terminal tabs, one per task/worktree

### Web and Mobile Sessions
Beyond the terminal, run additional sessions on claude.ai/code. Use:
- `&` command to background a session
- `--teleport` flag to switch contexts between local and web
- Claude iOS app to start sessions on the go, pick them up on desktop later

---

## 2. Model Selection

### Use Opus 4.5 with Thinking for Everything
Boris's reasoning: "It's the best coding model I've ever used, and even though it's bigger & slower than Sonnet, since you have to steer it less and it's better at tool use, it is almost always faster than using a smaller model in the end."

**The math:** Less steering + better tool use = faster overall results, even with a larger model.

---

## 3. Plan Mode

### Start Every Complex Task in Plan Mode
Press `shift+tab` to cycle to plan mode. Pour your energy into the plan so Claude can 1-shot the implementation.

**Workflow:** Plan mode -> Refine plan -> Auto-accept edits -> Claude 1-shots it

**Team patterns:**
- One person has one Claude write the plan, then spins up a second Claude to review it as a staff engineer
- The moment something goes sideways, switch back to plan mode and re-plan
- Explicitly tell Claude to enter plan mode for verification steps, not just for the build

"A good plan is really important to avoid issues down the line."

---

## 4. CLAUDE.md Best Practices

### Invest in Your CLAUDE.md
Share a single CLAUDE.md file for your repo, checked into git. The whole team should contribute.

**Key practice:** "Anytime we see Claude do something incorrectly we add it to the CLAUDE.md, so Claude knows not to do it next time."

**After every correction:** End with "Update your CLAUDE.md so you don't make that mistake again." Claude is eerily good at writing rules for itself.

**Advanced:** One engineer tells Claude to maintain a notes directory for every task/project, updated after every PR. They then point CLAUDE.md at it.

### @.claude in Code Reviews
Tag @.claude on PRs to add learnings to the CLAUDE.md as part of the PR itself. Use the Claude Code GitHub Action (`/install-github-action`) for this.

Example PR comment:
```
nit: use a string literal, not ts enum

@claude add to CLAUDE.md to never use enums,
always prefer literal unions
```

This is "Compounding Engineering" - Claude automatically updates the CLAUDE.md with the learning.

---

## 5. Skills & Slash Commands

### Create Your Own Skills
Create skills and commit them to git. Reuse across every project.

**Team tips:**
- If you do something more than once a day, turn it into a skill or command
- Build a `/techdebt` slash command and run it at the end of every session to find and kill duplicated code
- Set up a slash command that syncs 7 days of Slack, GDrive, Asana, and GitHub into one context dump
- Build analytics-engineer-style agents that write dbt models, review code, and test changes in dev

### Slash Commands for Inner Loops
Use slash commands for workflows you do many times a day. Commands are checked into git under `.claude/commands/` and shared with the team.

```
> /commit-push-pr
```

**Power feature:** Slash commands can include inline Bash to pre-compute info (like git status) for quick execution without extra model calls.

---

## 6. Subagents

### Use Subagents for Common Workflows
Think of subagents as automations for the most common PR workflows:

```
.claude/
  agents/
    build-validator.md
    code-architect.md
    code-simplifier.md
    oncall-guide.md
    verify-app.md
```

**Examples:**
- `code-simplifier` - Cleans up code after Claude finishes
- `verify-app` - Detailed instructions for end-to-end testing

### Leveraging Subagents
- Append "use subagents" to any request where you want Claude to throw more compute at the problem
- Offload individual tasks to subagents to keep your main agent's context window clean and focused
- Route permission requests to Opus 4.5 via a hook - let it scan for attacks and auto-approve the safe ones

---

## 7. Hooks

### PostToolUse Hooks for Formatting
Use a PostToolUse hook to auto-format Claude's code. While Claude generates well-formatted code 90% of the time, the hook catches edge cases to prevent CI failures.

```json
"PostToolUse": [
  {
    "matcher": "Write|Edit",
    "hooks": [
      {
        "type": "command",
        "command": "bun run format || true"
      }
    ]
  }
]
```

### Stop Hooks for Long-Running Tasks
For very long-running tasks, use an agent Stop hook for deterministic checks, ensuring Claude can work uninterrupted.

---

## 8. Permissions

### Pre-Allow Safe Permissions
Instead of `--dangerously-skip-permissions`, use `/permissions` to pre-allow common safe commands. Most are shared in `.claude/settings.json`.

For sandboxed environments, use `--permission-mode=dontAsk` or `--dangerously-skip-permissions` to avoid blocks.

---

## 9. MCP Integrations

### Tool Integrations
Claude Code uses your tools autonomously:
- Searches and posts to **Slack** (via MCP server)
- Runs **BigQuery** queries with bq CLI
- Grabs error logs from **Sentry**

```json
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "slack": {
      "type": "http",
      "url": "https://slack.mcp.anthropic.com/mcp"
    }
  }
}
```

### Data & Analytics
Ask Claude Code to use the "bq" CLI to pull and analyze metrics on the fly. Have a BigQuery skill checked into the codebase.

Boris's take: "Personally, I haven't written a line of SQL in 6+ months."

This works for any database that has a CLI, MCP, or API.

---

## 10. Prompting Tips

### Challenge Claude
- Say "Grill me on these changes and don't make a PR until I pass your test."
- Say "Prove to me this works" and have Claude diff behavior between main and your feature branch

### After a Mediocre Fix
Say: "Knowing everything you know now, scrap this and implement the elegant solution."

### Write Detailed Specs
Reduce ambiguity before handing work off. The more specific you are, the better the output.

**Key insight:** Don't accept the first solution. Push Claude to do better - it usually can.

---

## 11. Terminal Setup

### Recommended Tools
- **Ghostty** terminal - synchronized rendering, 24-bit color, proper unicode support
- Use `/statusline` to customize your status bar to always show context usage and current git branch

### Voice Dictation
Use voice dictation! You speak 3x faster than you type, and your prompts get way more detailed as a result. Hit `fn x2` on macOS.

---

## 12. Bug Fixing

### Let Claude Fix Bugs
Enable the Slack MCP, then paste a Slack bug thread into Claude and just say "fix." Zero context switching required.

Or just say "Go fix the failing CI tests." Don't micromanage how.

**Pro tip:** Point Claude at docker logs to troubleshoot distributed systems - it's surprisingly capable at this.

---

## 13. Long-Running Tasks

### Handle Long-Running Tasks
For very long-running tasks, ensure Claude can work uninterrupted:

**Options:**
- **(a)** Prompt Claude to verify with a background agent when done
- **(b)** Use an agent Stop hook for deterministic checks
- **(c)** Use the "ralph-wiggum" plugin (community idea by @GeoffreyHuntley)

For sandboxed environments, use `--permission-mode=dontAsk` or `--dangerously-skip-permissions` to avoid blocks.

---

## 14. Verification (The #1 Tip)

### Give Claude a Way to Verify Its Work
"Probably the most important thing to get great results out of Claude Code - give Claude a way to verify its work. If Claude has that feedback loop, it will 2-3x the quality of the final result."

**Verification varies by domain:**
- Bash commands
- Test suites
- Simulators
- Browser testing (Claude Chrome extension)

The key is giving Claude a way to close the feedback loop. Invest in domain-specific verification for optimal performance.

---

## 15. Learning with Claude

### Use Claude for Learning
- Enable "Explanatory" or "Learning" output style in /config to have Claude explain the *why* behind changes
- Have Claude generate visual HTML presentations explaining unfamiliar code
- Ask Claude to draw ASCII diagrams of new protocols and codebases
- Build a spaced-repetition learning skill: explain your understanding, Claude asks follow-ups to fill gaps

**Key takeaway:** Claude Code isn't just for writing code - it's a powerful learning tool when you configure it to explain and teach.

---

## 16. Terminal Configuration

### Configure Your Terminal
A few quick settings to make Claude Code feel right:

- **Theme:** Run `/config` to set light/dark mode
- **Notifications:** Enable notifications for iTerm2, or use a custom notifs hook
- **Newlines:** If you use Claude Code in an IDE terminal, Apple Terminal, Warp, or Alacritty, run `/terminal-setup` to enable shift+enter for newlines (so you don't need to type `\`)
- **Vim mode:** Run `/vim`

---

## 17. Effort Level

### Adjust Effort Level
Run `/model` to pick your preferred effort level:

- **Low** — less tokens & faster responses
- **Medium** — balanced behavior
- **High** — more tokens & more intelligence

Boris uses High for everything.

---

## 18. Plugins

### Install Plugins, MCPs, and Skills
Plugins let you install LSPs (now available for every major language), MCPs, skills, agents, and custom hooks.

Install a plugin from the official Anthropic plugin marketplace, or create your own marketplace for your company. Then, check the `settings.json` into your codebase to auto-add the marketplaces for your team.

Run `/plugin` to get started.

---

## 19. Custom Agents

### Create Custom Agents
Drop `.md` files in `.claude/agents`. Each agent can have a custom name, color, tool set, pre-allowed and pre-disallowed tools, permission mode, and model.

**Little-known feature:** Set the default agent used for the main conversation. Just set the `"agent"` field in your `settings.json` or use the `--agent` flag.

Run `/agents` to get started.

---

## 20. Permissions Management

### Pre-Approve Common Permissions
Claude Code uses a sophisticated permission system with prompt injection detection, static analysis, sandboxing, and human oversight.

Out of the box, we pre-approve a small set of safe commands. To pre-approve more, run `/permissions` and add to the allow and block lists. Check these into your team's `settings.json`.

**Wildcard syntax:** We support full wildcard syntax. Try `"Bash(bun run *)"` or `"Edit(/docs/**)"`.

---

## 21. Sandboxing

### Enable Sandboxing
Opt into Claude Code's open source sandbox runtime to improve safety while reducing permission prompts.

Run `/sandbox` to enable it. Sandboxing runs on your machine, and supports both file and network isolation.

**Modes:**
- Sandbox BashTool, with auto-allow
- Sandbox BashTool, with regular permissions
- No Sandbox

---

## 22. Status Line

### Add a Status Line
Custom status lines show up right below the composer. Show model, directory, remaining context, cost, and anything else you want to see while you work.

Everyone on the Claude Code team has a different statusline. Use `/statusline` to get started — Claude will generate one based on your `.bashrc`/`.zshrc`.

---

## 23. Keybindings

### Customize Your Keybindings
Every key binding in Claude Code is customizable. Run `/keybindings` to re-map any key. Settings live reload so you can see how it feels immediately.

Keybindings are stored in `~/.claude/keybindings.json`.

---

## 24. Hooks (Advanced)

### Set Up Hooks
Hooks are a way to deterministically hook into Claude's lifecycle. Use them to:

- Automatically route permission requests to Slack or Opus
- Nudge Claude to keep going when it reaches the end of a turn (you can even kick off an agent or use a prompt to decide whether Claude should keep going)
- Pre-process or post-process tool calls, e.g. to add your own logging

Ask Claude to add a hook to get started.

---

## 25. Spinner Verbs

### Customize Your Spinner Verbs
It's the little things that make CC feel personal. Ask Claude to customize your spinner verbs to add or replace the default list with your own verbs.

Check the `settings.json` into source control to share verbs with your team.

---

## 26. Output Styles

### Use Output Styles
Run `/config` and set an output style to have Claude respond using a different tone or format.

- **Explanatory** — great when getting familiar with a new codebase, to have Claude explain frameworks and code patterns as it works
- **Learning** — have Claude coach you through making code changes
- **Custom** — create your own output styles to adjust Claude's voice the way you like

---

## 27. Customize Everything

### Customize All the Things!
Claude Code is built to work great out of the box. When you do customize, check your `settings.json` into git so your team can benefit, too.

We support configuring for your codebase, for a sub-folder, for just yourself, or via enterprise-wide policies.

**By the numbers:** 37 settings and 84 env vars. Use the `"env"` field in your `settings.json` to avoid wrapper scripts.

---

## 28. Built-in Git Worktree Support

### Use `claude --worktree` for Isolation
Claude Code now has built-in git worktree support. Each agent gets its own worktree and can work independently, without interfering with other sessions.

```bash
# Start Claude in its own worktree
claude --worktree my_worktree

# Optionally launch in its own Tmux session too
claude --worktree my_worktree --tmux
```

**Desktop app:** Head to the Code tab in the Claude Desktop app and check the **worktree** checkbox.

### Subagents Support Worktrees
Subagents can also use worktree isolation to do more work in parallel. This is especially powerful for large batched changes and code migrations. Available in CLI, Desktop app, IDE extensions, web, and Claude Code mobile app.

**Example prompt:** "Migrate all sync io to async. Batch up the changes, and launch 10 parallel agents with worktree isolation. Make sure each agent tests its changes end to end, then have it put up a PR."

### Custom Agents with Worktree Isolation
Make subagents always run in their own worktree by adding `isolation: worktree` to your agent frontmatter:

```yaml
# .claude/agents/worktree-worker.md
---
name: worktree-worker
model: haiku
isolation: worktree
---
```

### Non-Git Source Control
Mercurial, Perforce, or SVN users can define `WorktreeCreate` and `WorktreeRemove` hooks in `settings.json` to benefit from isolation without Git.

---

## 29. /simplify — Improve Code Quality

Use parallel agents to improve code quality, tune code efficiency, and ensure CLAUDE.md compliance. Append `/simplify` to any prompt after making changes.

```
> hey claude make this code change then run /simplify
```

Boris uses this daily to shepherd PRs to production. The skill runs parallel agents that review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency — all in one pass.

---

## 30. /batch — Parallel Code Migrations

Interactively plan out code migrations, then execute in parallel using dozens of agents. Each agent runs with full isolation using git worktrees, testing its work before putting up a PR.

```
> /batch migrate src/ from Solid to React
```

You plan the migration interactively, then `/batch` fans out the work to parallel agents — each in its own worktree, each testing and creating a PR independently.

---

## 31. /loop — Schedule Recurring Tasks

Use `/loop` to schedule recurring tasks for up to 3 days at a time. Claude runs your prompt on an interval, handling long-running workflows autonomously.

```
> /loop babysit all my PRs. Auto-fix build issues and when comments come in, use a worktree agent to fix them
```

```
> /loop every morning use the Slack MCP to give me a summary of top posts I was tagged in
```

Use it for PR babysitting, Slack summaries, deploy monitoring, or any repeating workflow.

Learn more: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/scheduled-tasks

## 32. Code Review — Agents Hunt for Bugs

When a PR opens, Claude dispatches a team of agents to hunt for bugs. Anthropic built it for themselves first — code output per engineer is up 200% this year, and reviews were the bottleneck.

Each agent focuses on a different concern — logic errors, security issues, performance regressions — then posts inline comments directly on the PR. Boris personally used it for weeks before launch; it catches real bugs he wouldn't have noticed otherwise.

Source: https://x.com/bcherny/status/2031089411820228645

## 33. /btw — Ask Questions While Claude Works

A slash command for side-chain conversations while Claude is actively working. Single-turn, no tool calls, but has full context of the conversation.

```
> /btw what does the retry logic do?
```

Claude responds inline without stopping its work. Built by @ErikSchluntz as a side project — 1.5M views on the launch tweet.

Source: https://x.com/trq212/status/2031506296697131352

## 34. /effort — Max Reasoning Mode

Set effort to 'max' and Claude reasons for longer, using as many tokens as needed. Burns through usage limits faster, so you activate it per session.

```
> /effort max
```

Four levels: low, medium (default), high, max. Use 'max' for hard debugging, architecture decisions, or tricky code where you want Claude to really think it through.

Source: https://x.com/trq212/status/2032632596572811575

## 35. Remote Control — Spawn New Sessions

Run `claude remote-control` and spawn a new local session from the mobile app. Available on Max, Team, and Enterprise (v2.1.74+).

```bash
$ claude remote-control
# Open Claude mobile app → tap "Code" → start new session
```

Walk away from your desk, think of something, kick off a task from mobile — Claude runs on your machine.

Source: https://x.com/trq212/status/2032632597843779861

## 36. Voice Mode

Voice mode is now rolled out to 100% of users, including Claude Code Desktop and Cowork. Click the microphone icon and talk naturally.

Useful for hands-free coding, dictating complex requirements, or when you think faster than you type.

Source: https://x.com/trq212/status/2032632599429136753

## 37. Setup Scripts for Cloud Environments

Add a setup script in Claude Code on web and desktop. It runs before Claude Code launches on a cloud environment — install dependencies, configure settings, set env vars.

```bash
# Setup script (runs on new session start, skipped on resume):
#!/bin/bash
yarn install
```

Particularly useful for installing dependencies, settings, and configs before Claude starts working.

Source: https://x.com/trq212/status/2032632601064907037

## 38. claude --name — Name Your Sessions

Name your session at launch with the `--name` flag.

```bash
$ claude --name "auth-refactor"
```

Especially useful when juggling multiple worktrees or sessions — you can tell at a glance which session is doing what.

Source: https://x.com/trq212/status/2032632602629386348

## 39. Auto Session Naming After Plan Mode

After plan mode, Claude automatically names your session based on what you're working on. No manual naming needed.

Pairs well with `claude --name` — use `--name` when you know what you're doing upfront, let auto-naming handle it when you start by planning.

Source: https://x.com/trq212/status/2032632602629386348

## 40. /color — Customize Prompt Color

Change the color of the prompt input with `/color`. When you have 3-5 sessions open in different terminals, color-coding them makes it instantly clear which is which.

```
> /color
```

Source: https://x.com/trq212/status/2032632602629386348

## 41. PostCompact Hook

A new hook event that fires after Claude compresses its conversation context. Use it to re-inject critical instructions that might get lost during compaction, log when compaction happens, or trigger automation.

```json
"hooks": {
  "PostCompact": [{
    "matcher": "",
    "hooks": [{ "type": "command", "command": "echo 'Context was compacted'" }]
  }]
}
```

Source: https://x.com/trq212/status/2032632602629386348

---

## Quick Reference

| Tip | Key Action |
|-----|------------|
| Parallel work | Use git worktrees, 3-5 sessions |
| Model | Opus with thinking |
| Planning | Start in plan mode for complex tasks |
| CLAUDE.md | Update after every correction |
| Skills | Create for repeated workflows |
| Subagents | Offload to keep context clean |
| Hooks | Auto-format, lifecycle hooks, logging |
| Permissions | Pre-allow safe commands, wildcards |
| MCP | Integrate Slack, BigQuery, Sentry |
| Long-running | Use Stop hooks, background agents |
| Verification | Always give Claude a way to verify |
| Learning | Use Claude to explain and teach |
| Terminal | /config, /terminal-setup, /vim |
| Effort | /model to set Low/Medium/High |
| Plugins | /plugin for LSPs, MCPs, skills |
| Agents | .claude/agents, custom defaults |
| Sandboxing | /sandbox for file & network isolation |
| Status line | /statusline for custom info display |
| Keybindings | /keybindings to re-map any key |
| Spinners | Customize spinner verbs in settings |
| Output styles | Explanatory, learning, or custom |
| Customize | 37 settings, 84 env vars |
| Worktrees | `claude --worktree`, subagent isolation |
| /simplify | Parallel agents for code quality review |
| /batch | Parallel code migrations with worktree isolation |
| /loop | Schedule recurring tasks for up to 3 days |
| Code Review | Agent-powered PR reviews that catch real bugs |
| /btw | Ask questions mid-task without breaking flow |
| /effort | Max reasoning mode for deeper thinking |
| Remote Control | Spawn new sessions from mobile |
| Voice Mode | Talk to Claude Code on Desktop |
| Setup Scripts | Automate cloud environment setup |
| --name | Name sessions at launch |
| Auto Naming | Plan mode auto-names sessions |
| /color | Color-code prompt input per session |
| PostCompact | Hook for context compression events |

---

*Source: [howborisusesclaudecode.com](https://howborisusesclaudecode.com) - Tips from Boris Cherny's January 2026, February 2026, and March 2026 threads*
Let me first understand the v2 milestone issues and the current codebase state.

input.command: gh issue list --milestone "v2: Multi-Program & Sync" --json number,title,body,labels,state --jq '.[] | {number, title, state, labels: [.labels[].name], body: (.body[:300])}'
input.description: List v2 milestone issues with details

content.content:
{"body":"## Summary

All static content must be rendered from the program JSON definition, not hardcoded in templates. This is foundational — without it, program import (#4) and multi-program support (#5) are impossible.

## Content that must be data-driven

input.description: Get full bodies of all v2 issues

content.content:
## Summary

All static content must be rendered from the program JSON definition, not hardcoded in templates. This is foundational — without it, program import (#4) and multi-program support (#5) are impossible.

## Content that must be data-driven

- **Sessions & exercises**: names, sets/reps, coaching notes, video links, per-phase overrides
- **Week templates**: schedule options (4-day, 5-day, 6-day) with day-to-session mappings
- **Progressions**: phase-specific coaching adjustments per session
- **Skills**: names, timelines, requirements, gap analysis, milestones
- **Roadmap phases**: week ranges, summaries, gate tests
- **Benchmarks**: names, how-to, targets, retest frequencies
- **Foundations**: breathing protocol steps, deload rules and red flags
- **BODi integration**: recommended programs, hybrid schedules, integration rules (#10)
- **Resources**: YouTube channels, subreddits
- **Theme**: per-program and per-session color theming

## Approach

The program JSON schema (`schema/program.schema.json`) already defines the structure for most of this. Fragments read from the loaded program object — never from inline string literals. If content doesn't appear in the schema yet (BODi, resources, foundations), extend the schema first.

## Depends on

- Program JSON schema covering all content areas
- #10 (BODi integration — schema extension for external program recommendations)

## Blocks

- #4 (Program import/upload from JSON)
- #5 (Multiple program support)
---SEPARATOR---
Display actual workout data (sets, reps, weights) pulled from Hevy after completion.

Currently, the app only checks whether a workout was completed (binary). This enhancement adds:
- Detailed workout results on completed queue items
- Sets/reps/weight display for each exercise
- Comparison to prescribed sets from the program
- Volume tracking over time

Deferred from v1 because the core value is scheduling, not workout review (Hevy already does this well).
---SEPARATOR---
Add automatic workout completion detection instead of requiring manual refresh.

Currently, completion detection requires the user to open the app or tap "Refresh from Hevy." This enhancement adds:
- Polling interval for Hevy workout API (configurable)
- Or webhook endpoint if Hevy adds webhook support
- Background queue advancement on detected completions
- Push notification when a workout is detected as complete

Deferred from v1 to keep sync user-triggered only and avoid undocumented rate limit issues.
---SEPARATOR---
Support running multiple programs simultaneously or switching between them.

Currently, the app supports a single active program. This enhancement adds:
- Program library view
- Active program switching
- Per-program queue state
- Option to run complementary programs in parallel

Deferred from v1 to keep the data model simple (single active_program per user).
---SEPARATOR---
Allow users to upload their own program JSON files instead of using only the bundled program.

Currently, the program JSON is bundled with the Worker at build time. This enhancement adds:
- Upload UI for program JSON files
- Validation against schema/program.schema.json
- Storage of uploaded programs (D1 or R2)
- Program switching between uploaded and bundled programs

Deferred from v1 to simplify the initial implementation (single bundled program).

input.prompt:
Explore the hevy-planner codebase to understand the current architecture relevant to v2 planning. Specifically:

1. **Program loading**: How is the program JSON currently loaded and used? Look at `src/` for any `loadProgram`, program imports, or bundled program references. How does D1 storage work for programs?

2. **Rendering**: Find all places where content is hardcoded vs data-driven. Look in `src/fragments/` for hardcoded program names, exercise names, or session details that should come from program JSON.

3. **Hevy sync**: Look at `src/hevy/` and any sync-related routes. How does the current sync work? Is there webhook support? How are workouts matched to queue items?

4. **Queue system**: Look at `src/domain/` for queue and reflow logic. How is queue state managed per-program?

5. **Database schema**: Check `migrations/` for current D1 tables, especially anything related to programs, queue_items, and workout data storage.

6. **Route structure**: Check `src/index.ts` for all routes and how they're organized.

Be thorough - read the key files, not just list them. I need to understand the current state to plan v2 work.

content.text:
Perfect! Now I have a comprehensive understanding of the codebase. Let me create a summary document of my findings.

## Architecture Deep Dive: hevy-planner v1

Based on my thorough exploration of the codebase, here's the current architecture relevant to v2 planning:

### 1. **Program Loading & Storage**

**Current Implementation:**
- Programs are stored as **complete JSON blobs** in D1: `programs` table with columns `id`, `user_id`, `json_data`, `is_active`, `created_at`
- Loading happens via `loadProgram(db, userId)` which:
  1. Queries `getActiveProgram(db, userId)` → finds the newest active program
  2. Parses the JSON blob: `JSON.parse(row.json_data) as Program`
- Each user can have multiple program versions; only one is marked `is_active = 1`
- Program data loaded **per-request** (no static imports except the bundled default at line 46 of index.ts: `import defaultProgramJson from "../programs/mobility-joint-restoration.json"`)
- Setup flow (`handleSetup` in index.ts:478-648) validates, stores, and syncs the entire program in one transaction

**Key Path:** `/Users/ryanr/Code/hevy-planner/src/index.ts:80-84` (loadProgram)

### 2. **Rendering: Hardcoded vs Data-Driven**

**Current State: Nearly Fully Data-Driven**
- All fragments in `src/fragments/` take program data as parameters and render dynamically
- **Daily routine handling** is special:
  - Marked by `isDaily: boolean` on Routine type (src/types.ts:96)
  - Found via: `program.routines.find((r) => r.isDaily)` (index.ts:312, 867)
  - Hardcoded UI label: `"Daily"` color label with `var(--green)` (today.ts:22)
  - Hardcoded route: `/routine/daily` deep link (today.ts:27)

**Hardcoded Elements Found:**
1. Daily routine ID: route hardcodes `"daily"` in carsCard button: `@post('/api/push-hevy/daily')` (today.ts:19)
2. Label text: "Daily", "Next Session", "CARs only" (today.ts, reflow.ts:55)
3. Folder names: "Daily" folder for isDaily routines (hevy-sync.ts:117)

**All else is data-driven:**
- Exercise names, titles, descriptions → from exerciseTemplates + routines
- Routine counts, exercise counts → computed from program.routines
- Phase info → from program.progressions
- Upcoming sessions → computeUpcoming() algorithm uses template rhythm + queue state

**Key Paths:**
- `/Users/ryanr/Code/hevy-planner/src/fragments/today.ts:13-29` (carsCard with hardcoded "daily")
- `/Users/ryanr/Code/hevy-planner/src/fragments/routine-detail.ts:13-87` (fully data-driven)
- `/Users/ryanr/Code/hevy-planner/src/fragments/program.ts:79-130+` (routines section)

### 3. **Hevy Sync Architecture**

**Two-way Sync with Reconciliation:**

**Push (Setup + On-Demand):**
1. `handleSetup` (index.ts:478-648):
   - Creates or updates Hevy exercise templates (auto-match by name, then custom creation)
   - Creates folders via `getOrCreateRoutineFolder()`
   - Reconciles routines: checks D1 mappings first, then matches by title+folder in Hevy, then creates new
   - Stores 1:1 mappings in D1: `exercise_template_mappings` + `routine_mappings`
   
2. `handlePush` (index.ts:650-750):
   - For ad-hoc routine pushes (user clicks "Push to Hevy" on a detail page)
   - Uses existing mapping or creates new routine on-demand
   
3. Key functions:
   - `buildRoutinePayload()` — translates program routine + mappings → Hevy JSON
   - `computeFolderAssignments()` — decides folder per routine
   - `reconcileRoutines()` — decides create vs update
   - `autoMatchExercises()` — fuzzy-matches exercises by normalized name

**Pull (Hevy → App):**
1. `handlePull` (index.ts:808-889):
   - Fetches recent workouts from Hevy API
   - Skips workouts already matched to completed queue items (line 831-834)
   - Matches workouts to pending queue items by `hevy_routine_id` (matchCompletions logic)
   - Marks queue items completed with workout date (respects user timezone via `request.cf.timezone`)
   - Updates `daily_completed_date` on user if daily routine was logged
   
2. Completion matching:
   - Uses first-match-wins strategy: `if (!routineToItem.has(id))` (hevy-sync.ts:210)
   - Each workout matched to at most one queue item
   - Uses workout `start_time` converted to user timezone (index.ts:855-857)

**Webhook Support:** Not implemented yet. `getWebhookSubscription()` exists in Hevy client but not wired.

**Key Paths:**
- `/Users/ryanr/Code/hevy-planner/src/domain/hevy-sync.ts` — all payload builders, reconciliation, matching
- `/Users/ryanr/Code/hevy-planner/src/hevy/client.ts` — HevyClient API wrapper
- `/Users/ryanr/Code/hevy-planner/src/index.ts:808-889` — handlePull logic

### 4. **Queue System & Domain Logic**

**Queue Architecture:**
- Single `queue_items` table: `(id, user_id, routine_id, position, status, completed_date, hevy_routine_id, hevy_workout_id)`
- Generated at setup time via `generatePlaylist()` (queue.ts):
  1. Extracts main (non-daily) routines from selected week template
  2. Repeats them for N weeks: `for (let week = 0; week < weeks; week++)`
  3. Returns flat list with position 0, 1, 2, ...
- Queue items inserted via `insertQueueItems()` in batch (queries.ts:33-43)

**Next Routine Logic:**
- `getNextRoutine(items)` → finds first `status = 'pending'` by position (queue.ts:39-42)
- Shown as "hero card" on Today page

**Upcoming Sessions:**
- `computeUpcoming(pendingItems, template, routines, maxSessions)` (reflow.ts):
  1. Builds template rhythm from week template days
  2. Finds starting position in rhythm by matching first pending routine
  3. Iterates rhythm slots, consuming pending items for routine slots, inserting spacers for daily-only days
  4. Returns up to maxSessions items with type, title, color, exercise count
  5. Spacer type renders as "CARs only"

**Daily Routine (Special Case):**
- Tracked separately in `users.daily_completed_date` (not in queue)
- Rendered at top of Today page if `daily_completed_date !== today`
- Completion tied to workout logging in Hevy (not queue-based)

**Key Paths:**
- `/Users/ryanr/Code/hevy-planner/src/domain/queue.ts` — generatePlaylist, getNextRoutine, getCompletedRoutines
- `/Users/ryanr/Code/hevy-planner/src/domain/reflow.ts` — computeUpcoming (queue + template rhythm)
- `/Users/ryanr/Code/hevy-planner/src/index.ts:312-375` — Today page rendering combining daily + queue

### 5. **Database Schema**

**Current D1 Tables:**
1. `users` — `(id, hevy_api_key, active_program, template_id, start_date, created_at, daily_completed_date)`
2. `queue_items` — `(id, user_id, routine_id, position, status, completed_date, hevy_routine_id, hevy_workout_id)`
3. `skill_progress` — `(id, user_id, skill_id, milestone_index, updated_at)` (defined but unused in code)
4. `exercise_template_mappings` — `(user_id, program_template_id, hevy_template_id, is_custom)`
5. `routine_mappings` — `(user_id, program_routine_id, hevy_routine_id)`
6. `programs` — `(id, user_id, json_data, is_active, created_at)` with index on `(user_id, is_active, created_at DESC)`

**Foreign Keys:** All user-scoped tables reference `users(id)`. No queue → routine FK (routine_id is just a string reference).

**Key Paths:**
- `/Users/ryanr/Code/hevy-planner/migrations/0001_initial.sql` — core schema
- `/Users/ryanr/Code/hevy-planner/migrations/0003_programs_table.sql` — program storage
- `/Users/ryanr/Code/hevy-planner/migrations/0004_daily_completed_date.sql` — daily tracking

### 6. **Route Structure**

**Router in `/Users/ryanr/Code/hevy-planner/src/index.ts:111-256`:**

```
GET /                          → Today page (HTML shell + SSE)
  SSE content via handleTodaySSE

GET /progress                  → Progress page (HTML shell + SSE)
  SSE content via handleProgressSSE

GET /program                   → Program overview (HTML shell + SSE)
  SSE content via handleProgramSSE

GET /routine/:id               → Routine detail (HTML shell + SSE)
  SSE content via handleRoutineSSE

GET /programs/default.json     → Download bundled default program

POST /api/validate-program     → Validate JSON, return template cards (SSE fragment)

POST /api/setup/:templateId    → Main setup: store program, sync Hevy, create queue
  Optional body: {apiKey, startDate, templateId, programJson}

POST /api/push-hevy/:id        → Push single routine to Hevy (or open existing)

POST /api/pull                 → Pull workouts from Hevy, match to queue, mark complete

POST /api/complete/:id         → Manual queue item completion

POST /api/cleanup-routines     → Delete duplicate routines from Hevy
```

**Content Negotiation:** Uses `isSSERequest(request)` helper to check Accept header, serves HTML or SSE fragments accordingly (not separate `/sse/*` routes).

### 7. **Type System & Program Structure**

**Core Domain Types** (src/types.ts):
- `Program` — container with meta, exerciseTemplates[], routines[], weekTemplates[], progressions[], + optional roadmap, skills, benchmarks, foundations, resources, bodi
- `ExerciseTemplate` — `{id, title, type, equipmentCategory, primaryMuscleGroup, secondaryMuscleGroups?, notes?, videoURL?, searchTerms?, tags?, progressionByPhase?}`
- `Routine` — `{id, title, subtitle?, description?, color?, isDaily?, sortOrder?, folderGroup?, exercises[]}`
- `RoutineExercise` — `{exerciseTemplateId, sets, notes?}`
- `WeekTemplate` — `{id, name, description?, sortOrder?, days[]}`
- `TemplateDay` — `{dayOfWeek, label?, routineIDs?[]}`
- `Progression` — `{id, phaseName, weekRange, focus?, details?[], weekStart?, weekEnd?}`

**Storage Row Types:**
- `UserRow`, `QueueItemRow`, `ExerciseTemplateMappingRow`, `RoutineMappingRow`, `ProgramRow`

**Hevy Mapping Quirk:**
- Domain side uses camelCase: `exerciseType`, `equipmentCategory`, `primaryMuscleGroup`, `secondaryMuscleGroups`
- Wire (Hevy API) uses snake_case: `exercise_type`, `equipment_category`, `muscle_group`, `secondary_muscle_groups`
- Enum overrides in hevy-enums.ts:72-79 map extended values (foam_roller, pull_up_bar, hip_flexors, obliques) → valid Hevy enums

### 8. **Key Implementation Details**

**Authentication:**
- Via `getAuthenticatedUserOrDev()` (auth/access.ts) — checks CF access JWT or dev mode

**Error Handling:**
- Route handlers catch and return sseResponse with error message or 500
- Hevy API errors logged with status + body
- Rate limiting (429) throws RATE_LIMITED error

**Sets Parsing:**
- `parseSetsString()` (hevy-sync.ts:15-47) parses "3×8", "3×30 sec", "2×1 min" → HevySet[]
- Duration vs reps determined by presence of "sec" or "min" suffix

**Time Zone Handling:**
- User timezone from `request.cf.timezone` (Cloudflare header)
- Used to convert Hevy workout start_time → local date string (ISO YYYY-MM-DD format)
- "today" string computed via `todayString(timezone?)` helper

**HTML Escaping:**
- Central utils (src/utils/html.ts) with `escapeHtml()`, `escapeAttr()`, `truncate()`
- All dynamic values escaped in fragments

**SSE Response Format:**
- Uses Datastar for signal-driven updates
- Fragments return `patchElements()` calls with selectors + content
- Multiple fragments merged via `mergeFragments()` and end with `\n\n`

---

## Summary for v2 Planning

**Strengths:**
- Clean separation: domain logic (queue, reflow, sync) is pure functions in `src/domain/`
- Data-driven rendering: all fragments parameterized by program data
- Solid Hevy integration with reconciliation and 1:1 mappings
- Type-safe with TypeScript + JSON Schema validation
- Timezone-aware from the start

**Current Limitations (v2 candidates):**
1. **Hardcoded daily routine ID** — routes and buttons use "daily" string literal
2. **Program as monolithic JSON blob** — no schema versioning, upgrade path, or incremental updates
3. **No webhook support** — still polling-based (user clicks "Sync from Hevy")
4. **Queue is flat** — no sub-phases, branches, or dynamic reflow rules
5. **No skill progression tracking** — skill_progress table exists but unused
6. **No benchmark completion logging** — benchmarks are read-only metadata

**Architecture Ready for v2:**
- Program storage (D1 + JSON) supports versioning with minimal changes
- Mappings tables support multi-program scenarios (not yet used)
- Domain functions are pure and testable — easy to extend
- Fragment rendering pattern scales to new pages
- Hevy client methods are well-isolated for new API calls

All file paths are absolute and verified. Ready for architectural decisions on v2 scope.

Explore the hevy-planner codebase to understand the current architecture relevant to v2 planning. Specifically:

1. **Program loading**: How is the program JSON currently loaded and used? Look at `src/` for any `loadProgram`, program imports, or bundled program references. How does D1 storage work for programs?

2. **Rendering**: Find all places where content is hardcoded vs data-driven. Look in `src/fragments/` for hardcoded program names, exercise names, or session details that should come from program JSON.

3. **Hevy sync**: Look at `src/hevy/` and any sync-related routes. How does the current sync work? Is there webhook support? How are workouts matched to queue items?

4. **Queue system**: Look at `src/domain/` for queue and reflow logic. How is queue state managed per-program?

5. **Database schema**: Check `migrations/` for current D1 tables, especially anything related to programs, queue_items, and workout data storage.

6. **Route structure**: Check `src/index.ts` for all routes and how they're organized.

Be thorough - read the key files, not just list them. I need to understand the current state to plan v2 work.

content.text:
Perfect! Now I have a comprehensive understanding of the codebase. Let me create a summary document of my findings.

## Architecture Deep Dive: hevy-planner v1

Based on my thorough exploration of the codebase, here's the current architecture relevant to v2 planning:

### 1. **Program Loading & Storage**

**Current Implementation:**
- Programs are stored as **complete JSON blobs** in D1: `programs` table with columns `id`, `user_id`, `json_data`, `is_active`, `created_at`
- Loading happens via `loadProgram(db, userId)` which:
  1. Queries `getActiveProgram(db, userId)` → finds the newest active program
  2. Parses the JSON blob: `JSON.parse(row.json_data) as Program`
- Each user can have multiple program versions; only one is marked `is_active = 1`
- Program data loaded **per-request** (no static imports except the bundled default at line 46 of index.ts: `import defaultProgramJson from "../programs/mobility-joint-restoration.json"`)
- Setup flow (`handleSetup` in index.ts:478-648) validates, stores, and syncs the entire program in one transaction

**Key Path:** `/Users/ryanr/Code/hevy-planner/src/index.ts:80-84` (loadProgram)

### 2. **Rendering: Hardcoded vs Data-Driven**

**Current State: Nearly Fully Data-Driven**
- All fragments in `src/fragments/` take program data as parameters and render dynamically
- **Daily routine handling** is special:
  - Marked by `isDaily: boolean` on Routine type (src/types.ts:96)
  - Found via: `program.routines.find((r) => r.isDaily)` (index.ts:312, 867)
  - Hardcoded UI label: `"Daily"` color label with `var(--green)` (today.ts:22)
  - Hardcoded route: `/routine/daily` deep link (today.ts:27)

**Hardcoded Elements Found:**
1. Daily routine ID: route hardcodes `"daily"` in carsCard button: `@post('/api/push-hevy/daily')` (today.ts:19)
2. Label text: "Daily", "Next Session", "CARs only" (today.ts, reflow.ts:55)
3. Folder names: "Daily" folder for isDaily routines (hevy-sync.ts:117)

**All else is data-driven:**
- Exercise names, titles, descriptions → from exerciseTemplates + routines
- Routine counts, exercise counts → computed from program.routines
- Phase info → from program.progressions
- Upcoming sessions → computeUpcoming() algorithm uses template rhythm + queue state

**Key Paths:**
- `/Users/ryanr/Code/hevy-planner/src/fragments/today.ts:13-29` (carsCard with hardcoded "daily")
- `/Users/ryanr/Code/hevy-planner/src/fragments/routine-detail.ts:13-87` (fully data-driven)
- `/Users/ryanr/Code/hevy-planner/src/fragments/program.ts:79-130+` (routines section)

### 3. **Hevy Sync Architecture**

**Two-way Sync with Reconciliation:**

**Push (Setup + On-Demand):**
1. `handleSetup` (index.ts:478-648):
   - Creates or updates Hevy exercise templates (auto-match by name, then custom creation)
   - Creates folders via `getOrCreateRoutineFolder()`
   - Reconciles routines: checks D1 mappings first, then matches by title+folder in Hevy, then creates new
   - Stores 1:1 mappings in D1: `exercise_template_mappings` + `routine_mappings`
   
2. `handlePush` (index.ts:650-750):
   - For ad-hoc routine pushes (user clicks "Push to Hevy" on a detail page)
   - Uses existing mapping or creates new routine on-demand
   
3. Key functions:
   - `buildRoutinePayload()` — translates program routine + mappings → Hevy JSON
   - `computeFolderAssignments()` — decides folder per routine
   - `reconcileRoutines()` — decides create vs update
   - `autoMatchExercises()` — fuzzy-matches exercises by normalized name

**Pull (Hevy → App):**
1. `handlePull` (index.ts:808-889):
   - Fetches recent workouts from Hevy API
   - Skips workouts already matched to completed queue items (line 831-834)
   - Matches workouts to pending queue items by `hevy_routine_id` (matchCompletions logic)
   - Marks queue items completed with workout date (respects user timezone via `request.cf.timezone`)
   - Updates `daily_completed_date` on user if daily routine was logged
   
2. Completion matching:
   - Uses first-match-wins strategy: `if (!routineToItem.has(id))` (hevy-sync.ts:210)
   - Each workout matched to at most one queue item
   - Uses workout `start_time` converted to user timezone (index.ts:855-857)

**Webhook Support:** Not implemented yet. `getWebhookSubscription()` exists in Hevy client but not wired.

**Key Paths:**
- `/Users/ryanr/Code/hevy-planner/src/domain/hevy-sync.ts` — all payload builders, reconciliation, matching
- `/Users/ryanr/Code/hevy-planner/src/hevy/client.ts` — HevyClient API wrapper
- `/Users/ryanr/Code/hevy-planner/src/index.ts:808-889` — handlePull logic

### 4. **Queue System & Domain Logic**

**Queue Architecture:**
- Single `queue_items` table: `(id, user_id, routine_id, position, status, completed_date, hevy_routine_id, hevy_workout_id)`
- Generated at setup time via `generatePlaylist()` (queue.ts):
  1. Extracts main (non-daily) routines from selected week template
  2. Repeats them for N weeks: `for (let week = 0; week < weeks; week++)`
  3. Returns flat list with position 0, 1, 2, ...
- Queue items inserted via `insertQueueItems()` in batch (queries.ts:33-43)

**Next Routine Logic:**
- `getNextRoutine(items)` → finds first `status = 'pending'` by position (queue.ts:39-42)
- Shown as "hero card" on Today page

**Upcoming Sessions:**
- `computeUpcoming(pendingItems, template, routines, maxSessions)` (reflow.ts):
  1. Builds template rhythm from week template days
  2. Finds starting position in rhythm by matching first pending routine
  3. Iterates rhythm slots, consuming pending items for routine slots, inserting spacers for daily-only days
  4. Returns up to maxSessions items with type, title, color, exercise count
  5. Spacer type renders as "CARs only"

**Daily Routine (Special Case):**
- Tracked separately in `users.daily_completed_date` (not in queue)
- Rendered at top of Today page if `daily_completed_date !== today`
- Completion tied to workout logging in Hevy (not queue-based)

**Key Paths:**
- `/Users/ryanr/Code/hevy-planner/src/domain/queue.ts` — generatePlaylist, getNextRoutine, getCompletedRoutines
- `/Users/ryanr/Code/hevy-planner/src/domain/reflow.ts` — computeUpcoming (queue + template rhythm)
- `/Users/ryanr/Code/hevy-planner/src/index.ts:312-375` — Today page rendering combining daily + queue

### 5. **Database Schema**

**Current D1 Tables:**
1. `users` — `(id, hevy_api_key, active_program, template_id, start_date, created_at, daily_completed_date)`
2. `queue_items` — `(id, user_id, routine_id, position, status, completed_date, hevy_routine_id, hevy_workout_id)`
3. `skill_progress` — `(id, user_id, skill_id, milestone_index, updated_at)` (defined but unused in code)
4. `exercise_template_mappings` — `(user_id, program_template_id, hevy_template_id, is_custom)`
5. `routine_mappings` — `(user_id, program_routine_id, hevy_routine_id)`
6. `programs` — `(id, user_id, json_data, is_active, created_at)` with index on `(user_id, is_active, created_at DESC)`

**Foreign Keys:** All user-scoped tables reference `users(id)`. No queue → routine FK (routine_id is just a string reference).

**Key Paths:**
- `/Users/ryanr/Code/hevy-planner/migrations/0001_initial.sql` — core schema
- `/Users/ryanr/Code/hevy-planner/migrations/0003_programs_table.sql` — program storage
- `/Users/ryanr/Code/hevy-planner/migrations/0004_daily_completed_date.sql` — daily tracking

### 6. **Route Structure**

**Router in `/Users/ryanr/Code/hevy-planner/src/index.ts:111-256`:**

```
GET /                          → Today page (HTML shell + SSE)
  SSE content via handleTodaySSE

GET /progress                  → Progress page (HTML shell + SSE)
  SSE content via handleProgressSSE

GET /program                   → Program overview (HTML shell + SSE)
  SSE content via handleProgramSSE

GET /routine/:id               → Routine detail (HTML shell + SSE)
  SSE content via handleRoutineSSE

GET /programs/default.json     → Download bundled default program

POST /api/validate-program     → Validate JSON, return template cards (SSE fragment)

POST /api/setup/:templateId    → Main setup: store program, sync Hevy, create queue
  Optional body: {apiKey, startDate, templateId, programJson}

POST /api/push-hevy/:id        → Push single routine to Hevy (or open existing)

POST /api/pull                 → Pull workouts from Hevy, match to queue, mark complete

POST /api/complete/:id         → Manual queue item completion

POST /api/cleanup-routines     → Delete duplicate routines from Hevy
```

**Content Negotiation:** Uses `isSSERequest(request)` helper to check Accept header, serves HTML or SSE fragments accordingly (not separate `/sse/*` routes).

### 7. **Type System & Program Structure**

**Core Domain Types** (src/types.ts):
- `Program` — container with meta, exerciseTemplates[], routines[], weekTemplates[], progressions[], + optional roadmap, skills, benchmarks, foundations, resources, bodi
- `ExerciseTemplate` — `{id, title, type, equipmentCategory, primaryMuscleGroup, secondaryMuscleGroups?, notes?, videoURL?, searchTerms?, tags?, progressionByPhase?}`
- `Routine` — `{id, title, subtitle?, description?, color?, isDaily?, sortOrder?, folderGroup?, exercises[]}`
- `RoutineExercise` — `{exerciseTemplateId, sets, notes?}`
- `WeekTemplate` — `{id, name, description?, sortOrder?, days[]}`
- `TemplateDay` — `{dayOfWeek, label?, routineIDs?[]}`
- `Progression` — `{id, phaseName, weekRange, focus?, details?[], weekStart?, weekEnd?}`

**Storage Row Types:**
- `UserRow`, `QueueItemRow`, `ExerciseTemplateMappingRow`, `RoutineMappingRow`, `ProgramRow`

**Hevy Mapping Quirk:**
- Domain side uses camelCase: `exerciseType`, `equipmentCategory`, `primaryMuscleGroup`, `secondaryMuscleGroups`
- Wire (Hevy API) uses snake_case: `exercise_type`, `equipment_category`, `muscle_group`, `secondary_muscle_groups`
- Enum overrides in hevy-enums.ts:72-79 map extended values (foam_roller, pull_up_bar, hip_flexors, obliques) → valid Hevy enums

### 8. **Key Implementation Details**

**Authentication:**
- Via `getAuthenticatedUserOrDev()` (auth/access.ts) — checks CF access JWT or dev mode

**Error Handling:**
- Route handlers catch and return sseResponse with error message or 500
- Hevy API errors logged with status + body
- Rate limiting (429) throws RATE_LIMITED error

**Sets Parsing:**
- `parseSetsString()` (hevy-sync.ts:15-47) parses "3×8", "3×30 sec", "2×1 min" → HevySet[]
- Duration vs reps determined by presence of "sec" or "min" suffix

**Time Zone Handling:**
- User timezone from `request.cf.timezone` (Cloudflare header)
- Used to convert Hevy workout start_time → local date string (ISO YYYY-MM-DD format)
- "today" string computed via `todayString(timezone?)` helper

**HTML Escaping:**
- Central utils (src/utils/html.ts) with `escapeHtml()`, `escapeAttr()`, `truncate()`
- All dynamic values escaped in fragments

**SSE Response Format:**
- Uses Datastar for signal-driven updates
- Fragments return `patchElements()` calls with selectors + content
- Multiple fragments merged via `mergeFragments()` and end with `\n\n`

---

## Summary for v2 Planning

**Strengths:**
- Clean separation: domain logic (queue, reflow, sync) is pure functions in `src/domain/`
- Data-driven rendering: all fragments parameterized by program data
- Solid Hevy integration with reconciliation and 1:1 mappings
- Type-safe with TypeScript + JSON Schema validation
- Timezone-aware from the start

**Current Limitations (v2 candidates):**
1. **Hardcoded daily routine ID** — routes and buttons use "daily" string literal
2. **Program as monolithic JSON blob** — no schema versioning, upgrade path, or incremental updates
3. **No webhook support** — still polling-based (user clicks "Sync from Hevy")
4. **Queue is flat** — no sub-phases, branches, or dynamic reflow rules
5. **No skill progression tracking** — skill_progress table exists but unused
6. **No benchmark completion logging** — benchmarks are read-only metadata

**Architecture Ready for v2:**
- Program storage (D1 + JSON) supports versioning with minimal changes
- Mappings tables support multi-program scenarios (not yet used)
- Domain functions are pure and testable — easy to extend
- Fragment rendering pattern scales to new pages
- Hevy client methods are well-isolated for new API calls

All file paths are absolute and verified. Ready for architectural decisions on v2 scope.
Good exploration. I have a clear picture of the 5 issues, their dependencies, and current architecture. Let me design the execution plan.

input.prompt:
Design an execution plan for the v2 milestone of hevy-planner, following Boris Cherny's Claude Code workflow principles: parallel worktrees, plan-then-1-shot, verification at every step, and /simplify after changes.

## v2 Issues (5 total)

### #9 — Data-driven rendering
All static content rendered from program JSON, not hardcoded. Foundational — blocks #4 and #5.
Current state: Nearly fully data-driven already. Remaining hardcoded items:
- Daily routine ID: routes/buttons use "daily" string literal (today.ts:19, index.ts route `/api/push-hevy/daily`)
- Label text: "Daily", "Next Session", "CARs only" (today.ts, reflow.ts:55)
- Folder names: "Daily" folder for isDaily routines (hevy-sync.ts:117)
- Daily routine color: hardcoded `var(--green)` (today.ts:22)
Key files: src/fragments/today.ts, src/domain/reflow.ts, src/domain/hevy-sync.ts, src/index.ts

### #7 — Workout detail display (sets/reps/weights from Hevy)
Show actual workout data on completed queue items. Currently binary (done/not done).
Needs: Hevy workout data storage, display in fragments, comparison to prescribed sets.
Key files: src/hevy/client.ts, src/storage/queries.ts, src/fragments/today.ts, src/index.ts (handlePull)

### #6 — Webhook-based auto-sync
Replace manual "Sync from Hevy" with webhooks. Hevy MCP tools include create-webhook-subscription, get-webhook-subscription, delete-webhook-subscription, get-workout-events.
Needs: webhook endpoint route, event processing, queue advancement.
Key files: src/index.ts, src/domain/hevy-sync.ts, src/hevy/client.ts

### #4 — Program import/upload from JSON
Upload UI for program JSON, validate against schema, store in D1.
Currently: only bundled program loaded at setup. D1 `programs` table already stores JSON blobs.
Needs: upload UI fragment, validation endpoint, program storage, re-setup flow.
Key files: src/index.ts, src/fragments/, src/storage/queries.ts, schema/program.schema.json

### #5 — Multiple program support
Program library, active switching, per-program queue state.
Currently: single active_program per user. Queue items reference routine_id (string from program JSON).
Needs: program list page, switching logic, queue scoped to program, parallel program option.
Key files: All of the above + new fragments, domain logic changes.

## Dependency Graph
```
#9 (data-driven) ──→ #4 (import) ──→ #5 (multi-program)
#7 (workout details) — independent
#6 (webhooks) — independent
```

## Current Architecture Context
- Cloudflare Workers + D1 + Datastar SSE
- Programs stored as JSON blobs in D1 `programs` table
- Queue: flat `queue_items` table with position-based ordering
- Hevy sync: push (setup) + pull (manual button), reconciliation via D1 mappings
- All domain logic pure functions in src/domain/
- Fragments return HTML strings with Datastar attributes
- 47 tests passing
- Content negotiation via Accept header (SSE vs HTML)

## Boris Method Requirements
1. **Parallel worktrees**: Identify which issues can be worked simultaneously
2. **Plan → 1-shot**: Each issue needs enough detail for a single implementation pass
3. **Verification**: Define how to test each change (tests, MCP tools, manual checks)
4. **/simplify**: Run after each issue to clean up

Design the execution plan with:
- Wave structure (what runs in parallel vs sequential)
- For each issue: scope, key changes, files modified, verification steps
- Migration needs (D1 schema changes)
- Risk areas and gotchas

content.content:
1→# Product Spec
     2→
     3→## What Is This?
     4→
     5→A training companion that adds the planning intelligence Hevy doesn't have. It tells you what to do today, automatically adjusts your schedule when you miss a workout, tracks long-term skill progression, and manages benchmark testing across a multi-phase roadmap — all built around programs you can define, import, and share.
     6→
     7→Hevy handles the workout itself: exercise logging, Apple Watch, sets/reps, PRs, history. This companion handles everything upstream and downstream: scheduling, reflow, phase management, and program definition.
     8→
     9→It runs as a web app hosted on Cloudflare Workers, accessed from any browser or as a PWA on your phone's home screen. It syncs with Hevy via the Hevy API.
    10→
    11→### Goals
    12→
    13→- Show the user exactly what to do today, in order, with coaching context
    14→- Automatically reflow the schedule when a workout is missed
    15→- Track progress across a multi-phase roadmap (weeks to months to a year)
    16→- Gate advancement between phases on measurable benchmarks
    17→- Track long-term skill goals (muscle-up, handstand, etc.) with milestone progression
    18→- Push today's routine to Hevy so it's ready when you open the app
    19→- Pull completion data from Hevy to advance the queue
    20→- Define programs via an open JSON schema that anyone can create and share
    21→
    22→### Non-Goals (v1)
    23→
    24→- Replacing Hevy for workout logging (sets, reps, rest timers, PRs)
    25→- Building native iOS or watchOS apps
    26→- AI-generated programming or adaptive difficulty
    27→- Social features
    28→
    29→---
    30→
    31→## How It Works With Hevy
    32→
    33→### Sync Model
    34→
    35→```
    36→   Companion (Cloudflare)              Hevy
    37→   ─────────────────────              ────
    38→   Program loaded from JSON
    39→   Queue generated from template
    40→   "Today: CARs + Session B"
    41→            │
    42→            ├──push routine──────►  Routine appears in Hevy
    43→            │                       User opens Hevy, does workout
    44→            │                       Logs sets/reps on phone or Watch
    45→            │
    46→            ◄──pull completion───  Workout marked complete
    47→            │
    48→   Queue advances
    49→   Reflow if needed
    50→   Next day's routine ready
    51→```
    52→
    53→### Hevy API Integration
    54→
    55→The [Hevy API](https://api.hevyapp.com/docs/) (requires Pro subscription) provides:
    56→
    57→- **Routines**: Create, update, and list workout routines. The companion pushes today's session as a Hevy routine.
    58→- **Workouts**: List completed workouts with full exercise/set detail. The companion polls for completions to advance the queue.
    59→- **Exercises**: List available exercises and exercise templates. Used to map program exercises to Hevy's exercise library.
    60→- **Routine Folders**: Organize routines by program or phase.
    61→
    62→### Sync Triggers
    63→
    64→- **On page load**: Pull recent workouts from Hevy, reconcile with queue state
    65→- **On "Push to Hevy" tap**: Create or update today's routine in Hevy
    66→- **On manual refresh**: Re-pull from Hevy to check for new completions
    67→- **Future**: Webhook or polling interval for automatic sync

   280→
   281→## User Flows
   282→
   283→### First Launch / Setup
   284→
   285→1. Open the app URL in browser (or install as PWA)
   286→2. Connect Hevy: enter API key (from Hevy Settings > Developer)
   287→3. Load a program: select bundled program or import from JSON file
   288→4. Pick a start date (defaults to today)
   289→5. Pick a schedule template (4-day, 5-day, 6-day)
   290→6. Land on Today screen with the first day's queue populated
   291→
   292→### Daily Use
   293→
   294→1. Open companion → Today screen shows queue (CARs + main session)
   295→2. Review session detail and coaching notes if needed
   296→3. Tap "Push to Hevy" → routine appears in Hevy
   297→4. Open Hevy on phone or Watch, do the workout, log sets/reps
   298→5. Return to companion (or it auto-syncs) → workout detected as complete
   299→6. Queue advances, next item updates, week overview reflects completion
   300→
   301→### Missed Workout
   302→
   303→1. It's Thursday. Wednesday's Session B was not completed.
   304→2. Open the companion Thursday morning.
   305→3. Today screen shows Thursday's normal schedule (CARs + strength note).
   306→4. Session B has been re-enqueued to the first open day this week.
   307→5. Week overview shows Wednesday as missed.
   308→6. Friday proceeds normally with its scheduled session.
   309→
   310→### Benchmark Testing
   311→
   312→1. Benchmarks screen shows "Wall Dorsiflexion Test — due this week"
   313→2. User performs the test, taps "Log Result", enters measurement
   314→3. Result is saved, trend updates, gate test status recalculated
   315→4. If all gate tests for current roadmap phase pass, user is prompted to advance
   316→
   317→### Phase Advancement
   318→
   319→1. All Phase 1 gate benchmarks pass
   320→2. Roadmap screen shows "Phase 1 complete — ready to advance?"
   321→3. User confirms → Phase 2 becomes active
   322→4. New sessions/exercises are added to the queue from the Phase 2 program definition
   323→5. Schedule template may update (or user is prompted to choose a new one)
   324→
   325→---
   326→
   327→## Queue & Reflow Rules
   328→
   329→Same rules as before — the queue is the core scheduling engine:
   330→
   331→1. Each day, the queue is populated from the active template. **Daily sessions** are inserted first, followed by the day's scheduled session(s), in template order.
   332→
   333→2. The user works through items in sequence. The **next item** is always the first incomplete item in today's queue.
   334→
   335→3. When Hevy reports an item as **completed**, it's marked done and the queue advances.
   336→
   337→4. **Daily sessions have special reset behavior**: never reflowed when missed, just dropped. A fresh daily entry is inserted at the top of the next day's queue.
   338→
   339→5. **Non-daily sessions follow reflow rules**: when a scheduled date passes without completion, the session is re-enqueued after the remaining scheduled sessions for the week. If no open day remains, it's dropped for the week.
   340→
   341→6. At the **end of each week**, any remaining skipped sessions are dropped. The next week's queue is generated fresh from the template.
   342→
   343→---
   344→
   345→## Data Schema
   346→
   347→Programs are defined via the JSON Schema in `schema/program.schema.json`. The schema covers:
   348→
   349→- **Program metadata**: title, author, duration, tags
   350→- **Sessions**: named groups of exercises, with an `isDaily` flag for daily-reset behavior
   351→- **Exercises**: name, sets/reps, coaching notes, video links, cross-session tags, per-phase overrides
   352→- **Week templates**: multiple schedule options (e.g., 4-day, 5-day, 6-day)
   353→- **Progressions**: phase-based coaching adjustments within the program
   354→- **Roadmap phases**: long-term phases with gate tests that reference benchmarks
   355→- **Skills**: long-term goals with milestones and timelines
   356→- **Benchmarks**: measurable assessments with targets and retest frequencies
   357→- **Foundations**: protocols (breathing, bracing) that apply across the program
   358→- **Theme**: optional per-program and per-session color theming
   359→
   360→See `schema/program.schema.json` for the full definition.
   361→
   362→### User Data (persisted in D1)
   363→
   364→```
   365→UserState
   366→  userID: string
   367→  activeProgramID: string
   368→  activeTemplateID: string
   369→  startDate: date
   370→  currentRoadmapPhaseID: string
   371→  hevyAPIKey: string (encrypted)
   372→
   373→QueueItem
   374→  sessionID: string
   375→  scheduledDate: date
   376→  position: int
   377→  isDaily: bool
   378→  status: pending | ready | completed | skipped
   379→  hevyRoutineID: string?
   380→  hevyWorkoutID: string?
   381→
   382→BenchmarkResult
   383→  benchmarkID: string
   384→  recordedAt: datetime
   385→  value: string
   386→  notes: string?
   387→
   388→CompletedWorkout
   389→  sessionID: string
   390→  completedAt: datetime
   391→  hevyWorkoutID: string
   392→  durationSeconds: int
   393→```
   394→
   395→### Hevy Exercise Mapping
   396→
   397→Programs define exercises by name. The companion maps these to Hevy's exercise library when pushing routines. A mapping table in D1 stores confirmed matches:
   398→
   399→```
   400→ExerciseMapping
   401→  programExerciseID: string
   402→  hevyExerciseID: string
   403→  confirmedByUser: bool
   404→```
   405→
   406→On first push, the companion attempts to auto-match by name. Unmatched exercises are flagged for the user to resolve manually.
   407→
   408→---
   409→
   410→## Authentication
   411→
   412→The app is protected by [Cloudflare Access](https://www.cloudflare.com/zero-trust/products/access/), which handles authentication at the edge before any request reaches the Worker. There is no application-level login flow, session management, or password storage.
   413→
   414→**How it works**: Cloudflare Access acts as an identity-aware reverse proxy. Users authenticate through a configured identity provider (email OTP, GitHub, Google, etc.) and receive a signed JWT. The Worker validates this token on every request — unauthenticated requests never reach application code.
   415→
   416→**Access policy**: An allowlist of permitted email addresses (starting with the owner's). Adding new users means adding an email to the Cloudflare Access policy — no application changes required.
   417→
   418→**What the Worker does**:
   419→
   420→- Validates the `Cf-Access-Jwt-Assertion` header on every request
   421→- Extracts the authenticated user identity from the token claims
   422→- Uses the identity to scope all D1 queries (each user sees only their own data)
   423→- Rejects requests with missing or invalid tokens (403)
   424→
   425→**What the Worker does NOT do**:
   426→
   427→- Store passwords or credentials
   428→- Manage login/logout sessions
   429→- Implement OAuth flows
   430→- Handle user registration
   431→
   432→**Cost**: Cloudflare Access is free for up to 50 users. This is more than sufficient for personal use and early open-source adoption.
   433→
   434→---
   435→
   436→## Settings
   437→
   438→- **Hevy Connection**: API key management, sync status, last sync time
   439→- **Template**: Switch between schedule templates (regenerates future queue items)
   440→- **Start Date**: Adjust if needed (recalculates current week and phase)
   441→- **Program**: View active program, import new program from JSON
   442→- **Reset Program**: Start over from week 1 (confirmation required)
   443→
   444→---
   445→
   446→## Prerequisites & Costs
   447→
   448→### What You Need
   449→
   450→- **Hevy Pro subscription** ($9.99/month or $49.99/year) — required for API access. Get your API key from [Hevy Settings > Developer](https://hevy.com/settings?developer). [Hevy app](https://www.hevyapp.com/).
   451→- **Cloudflare account** (free tier is sufficient to start) — [Sign up](https://dash.cloudflare.com/sign-up).
   452→- **Node.js 18+** — for local development and the Wrangler CLI.
   453→- **Wrangler CLI** — Cloudflare's deployment tool (`npm install -g wrangler`).
   454→
   455→### Cloudflare Costs
   456→
   457→The free tier covers most personal use:
   458→
   459→| Resource | Free Tier | Notes |
   460→|----------|-----------|-------|
   461→| Workers requests | 100,000/day | More than enough for a personal app |
   462→| D1 database | 5 GB storage, 5M rows read/day | Workout history for years |
   463→| KV (if used) | 100,000 reads/day | Not required for v1 |
   464→| Custom domain | Bring your own | Optional; `*.workers.dev` subdomain is free |
   465→
   466→**Estimated cost for personal use: $0/month** beyond the Hevy Pro subscription.
   467→
   468→If usage grows (multiple users, heavy API polling), the Workers Paid plan is $5/month and covers 10M requests/month with higher D1 limits.
   469→
   470→### Hevy API Limits
   471→
   472→The Hevy API documentation does not publish explicit rate limits. The app should implement conservative backoff/retry logic and cache aggressively. For a single user syncing a few times per day, rate limits are unlikely to be an issue.
   473→
   474→---
   475→
   476→## Decisions Log
   477→
   478→| # | Decision | Rationale |
   479→|---|----------|-----------|
   480→| 1 | Companion to Hevy, not a standalone tracker | Hevy already has native apps, Watch support, exercise logging, PRs. Don't rebuild what works. |
   481→| 2 | Cloudflare Workers + Datastar | The entire product is a web app. Datastar's SSE-driven hypermedia is a natural fit. No native apps needed. |
   482→| 3 | TypeScript on Workers | First-class Cloudflare support. Datastar TS SDK works directly. Claude knows it deeply for agent-assisted builds. |
   483→| 4 | D1 for persistence | Relational queries for queue state, workout history, benchmark trends. SQLite at the edge. |
   484→| 5 | Programs defined via JSON Schema | Open, shareable, community-extensible. Anyone can create a program. |
   485→| 6 | Daily sessions are queue items that reset daily | Unified queue model. Daily items always appear first, never reflowed. |
   486→| 7 | Hevy API for sync, not scraping or manual entry | Official API with routine and workout CRUD. Requires Pro subscription. |
   487→| 8 | PWA for mobile access | No App Store needed. Install from browser. Works on any device with a browser. |
   488→| 9 | Cloudflare Access for authentication | Zero application auth code. Identity handled at the edge. Free for up to 50 users. |
   489→
